Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most people think hackathons are about who writes the cleanest code. After building KindCents with my team, Quadcore, at Devthon 3.0, I realized that is a total lie.
Hackathons are actually about one thing: shipping.
As an IT undergrad currently grinding through OOP and Data Structures, I used to be a “documentation first” student. I hated the idea of vibe coding or taking shortcuts. But when the clock is ticking, a perfect architecture is useless if it never actually deploys.
If you want to win, or at least finish, here is how you actually hack a hackathon.

1. Solve a problem that actually hurts
The biggest mistake you can make is solving a problem just for the sake of the hackathon. For KindCents, we focused on the lack of transparency in donations. It was a pain factor we felt deeply; Especially during past floods, when the lack of clear financial tracking made people hesitate to contribute.
When you solve a problem that resonates with you, the motivation changes. Even if you don’t get anything in return, the satisfaction of building a solution is enough to keep you going. If you don’t care about the problem, you will probably quit when the code starts breaking at 2 AM.
2. Kill your inner perfectionist
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I used to be a total hater when it came to “vibe coding.” To me, the idea of just throwing code together and hoping for the best felt like a guaranteed way to break everything. But in a competition, you don’t have weeks to architect: you have hours.
Instead of traditional tutorials, we leaned hard into Claude. We used AI not just to write snippets, but to architect the flow of KindCents as we built it. It is high intensity learning that prioritizes results. Using AI to debug at 2 AM isn’t a replacement for a semester of lectures, but it is the only way to turn an idea into a URL before the deadline hits.
3. Don’t just copy-paste
Using AI helps you move fast, but you still have to know what the code is actually doing. Never just blind copy paste. If a judge asks how your data is being pulled from Firestore and you can’t walk them through the logic, you’re done. We used Claude to speed up the process, but we made sure we understood every component we integrated. If you can’t explain it, you didn’t really build it.
4. Choose a “cheat code” tech stack
Don’t try to build a custom backend from scratch. We used React 19, Vite, and Firebase for KindCents for a few practical reasons:

• Firestore: It allowed us to handle data syncing without the overhead of traditional SQL setups.

• Authentication: Firebase handled the secure login logic so we could focus on the actual donation tracking.

• Stripe: We integrated Stripe for payments because we needed a secure, reliable way to handle transactions without having to build a custom payment gateway from scratch.
5. The Figma vs. Reality Gap
We actually had a Figma submission a week before the actual site was due. Having those wireframes was a lifesaver, but it also created a massive challenge: we had to make the real site live up to the “perfect” design.
The initial goal was simple: make the site exist.
We used our notebook sketches to bridge the gap between the Figma design and the actual code. We didn’t worry about the polish or the extra features until we had a live URL. Once the foundation was solid, then we started layering on the cool functionality as we went. If the site doesn’t load, the beautiful design doesn’t matter.
6. Testing on a Budget
You can’t ship a broken product, but you also don’t have time for manual testing. We used Bugsmash to catch our errors. Since we were on a student budget, we hopped on the 1 month free trial to get the job done. It is a classic hackathon move: use the best professional tools available to get across the finish line.
7. Focus on the “wow” factor
When we won Most Popular Team, it wasn’t because our code was a masterpiece. It was because we had a working, hosted product people could actually interact with.
While the judges definitely look at your code and folder structure to see if you actually built a solid foundation, the crowd cares that they can click a button and see a donation update in real-time. You need both; a clean enough setup to pass the technical check, and a “wow” factor to win the room.
